subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s degree in International Relations
Completed coursework in data analysis, security studies, and political science
Participated in a university internship with a government security agency
Attended workshops on open-source intelligence (OSINT) gathering
Took online courses in cyber threat analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Intelligence Analyst
Responsible for gathering, analyzing, and interpreting security-related data
Prepares intelligence briefings and risk assessment reports for decision-makers
Collaborates regularly with law enforcement and cybersecurity teams
Monitors geopolitical events and emerging threats

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the security and intelligence sector
Focuses on cyber and physical threat analysis
Engages with both public and private sector organizations
Industry overlaps with law enforcement, government, and corporate security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized intelligence consultancy (~150 employees)
Organization has a hybrid work environment (remote and on-site)
Team consists of 10-12 analysts with varying specialties
Company culture emphasizes collaboration and confidentiality

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company provides intelligence analysis services to government and corporate clients
Specializes in threat assessment and risk mitigation
Operates in the national security and critical infrastructure protection market
Serves sectors such as defense, energy, and finance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as an Intelligence Analyst
Joined the current organization after graduating from university
Previously completed a 6-month internship in a similar role
Experience includes both training and on-the-job learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Positioned at the entry to early-career level
Reports to a senior intelligence analyst or team lead
Occasionally mentors new interns or junior analysts
Contributes to team projects but does not lead major initiatives

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Collects and reviews intelligence from open sources, databases, and internal reports
Analyzes data to identify patterns, anomalies, or emerging threats
Drafts concise intelligence reports and summaries for internal and external stakeholders
Participates in cross-team meetings to discuss ongoing investigations
Keeps up-to-date with geopolitical events and security technology advancements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 40% of time on data gathering and initial review
Allocates 30% of the day to in-depth data analysis and pattern recognition
Devotes 15% of time to report writing and presentation preparation
Spends 10% in meetings and collaborative discussions
Uses remaining 5% for professional development and news monitoring

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates with moderate autonomy in daily data analysis and reporting
Major intelligence findings are reviewed by a supervisor before escalation
Can independently prioritize daily tasks based on threat level and urgency
Participates in group decision-making for larger investigations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Data collection and initial review are straightforward due to established procedures
Organizing datasets in Excel or Google Sheets comes naturally
Summarizing key findings for internal briefs is comfortable
Communicating with team members via secure messaging is routine

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Detecting subtle, non-obvious patterns in large, unstructured datasets
Synthesizing conflicting intelligence sources into a coherent narrative
Handling sensitive or time-critical incidents with incomplete information
Producing high-impact reports for senior decision-makers on short notice

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Bulk data cleaning and de-duplication in spreadsheets
Routine scanning of news feeds and alerts for specified keywords
Compiling preliminary lists of relevant incidents or entities
Formatting and templating standard intelligence reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys deep-dive analysis and drawing connections across data sources
Finds collaborative problem-solving sessions stimulating
Prefers preparing presentations over repetitive data entry
Considers manual data cleaning to be tedious and time-consuming
Enjoys monitoring relevant news and global events for emerging risks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Lack of integration between data sources requires manual consolidation
Repetitive formatting of reports consumes significant time
Occasional delays due to waiting for access to restricted information
Data quality issues (e.g., inconsistent formats) slow down analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive when working on intellectually challenging analysis
Enjoyment increases when tasks match skillset, especially in pattern recognition
Repetitive, low-skill tasks are less enjoyable and sometimes reduce focus
Learning new skills (e.g., advanced OSINT techniques) boosts motivation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally enthusiastic about experimenting with new analytical tools
Views technology adoption as essential for staying relevant in the field
Finds learning new tools rewarding when they improve workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software with user-friendly interfaces
Occasionally needs time to adjust to complex or specialized platforms
Open to learning through hands-on practice and guided tutorials

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Regularly sets aside time for self-driven learning on new tools
Willing to attend webinars or workshops during less busy periods
Prioritizes training on technologies with clear relevance to daily tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the promise of increased efficiency and accuracy
Heavy workload can sometimes delay adoption of new tools
More likely to invest in learning if peers or supervisors endorse a technology
Skeptical of tools that lack clear documentation or support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peer recommendations strongly influence willingness to try new software
Management encouragement accelerates adoption of organization-approved tools
Likes exchanging tips and best practices with colleagues
More likely to pursue new tools when success stories are shared internally

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Excel and Google Sheets daily for data organization and analysis
Regularly utilizes OSINT tools such as Maltego and Shodan
Checks threat intelligence platforms like Recorded Future or Anomali each day
Prepares reports and presentations using Microsoft Word and PowerPoint
Communicates via encrypted messaging apps such as Signal

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Excel and Google Sheets for manual data entry and tracking
OSINT platforms for gathering information from public sources
i2 Analyst’s Notebook for occasional link analysis and mapping
Secure corporate email for sharing sensitive documents

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with primary threat intelligence tools recognized in the industry
Familiar with both basic and advanced features of Excel
Has working knowledge of mapping and visualization software
Regularly reads industry updates to stay informed about new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Manual data transfer is needed between some OSINT tools and spreadsheets
Report formatting inconsistencies occur when moving between Word and PowerPoint
Limited automated integration between threat intelligence platforms and internal databases
Occasional compatibility issues between mapping tools and exported data formats

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands foundational terms like NLP, classification, and clustering
Familiar with the concept of large language models (LLMs)
Can explain the basics of how AI models process text data
Recognizes the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and other LLMs for drafting and summarizing reports
Employs AI-powered features in Recorded Future for automated threat detection
Occasionally tries Google Bard for rapid information synthesis
Tests internal AI chatbots for data query and extraction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs daily for generating report drafts and summaries
Uses AI tools to scan news feeds and flag potentially relevant incidents
Employs automated entity extraction to identify key actors in datasets
Relies on AI to generate visualizations or quick data insights

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI has expedited initial draft writing, reducing turnaround time by up to 30%
Automated flagging occasionally produces false positives, requiring manual review
Learned that LLM-generated content must be carefully validated for accuracy
AI-driven data cleaning improved efficiency, but struggled with messy, unstructured inputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Company offers occasional webinars and internal guides on AI tool usage
Peers share best practices for prompt engineering in team chats
Access to a knowledge base with AI use case examples
Mentorship from a senior analyst who specializes in AI applications

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for complex tasks
Uses AI independently for simple, routine queries
Finds interactive, iterative prompting yields better results for nuanced analysis
Adjusts interaction style based on the task's ambiguity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for drafting and automating repetitive work
Engages AI as a brainstorming partner when exploring new angles
Prefers to retain the final judgment and critical decisions
Occasionally treats AI as a co-worker for quick fact-checking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with AI-driven workflows if they save time
Actively seeks out pilot projects to test new AI capabilities
Evaluates new AI processes for transparency and reliability before full adoption
Advocates for a balanced approach, combining AI and human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for flexible, iterative work
Uses command-based prompts for well-defined, structured tasks
Finds conversational AI more user-friendly for brainstorming and exploration
Switches between modes depending on the complexity of the request

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks, such as threat assessment, depend on context-specific expertise
Understanding geopolitical nuances requires human judgment and experience
AI outputs are most valuable when paired with analyst domain knowledge
Tacit knowledge is essential for interpreting ambiguous or incomplete data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine summarization and pattern detection with review
Does not fully rely on AI outputs without manual verification for critical tasks
Confidence in AI increases when outputs are explainable and consistent
Skeptical of AI-generated insights when data sources are unclear

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Favors AI automation for repetitive data processing and initial triage
Believes human analysts should interpret and validate AI findings
Supports AI-human collaboration for complex, high-stakes analysis
Sees the best value in AI as a force multiplier, not a replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation in low-risk, standardized reporting tasks
Finds high automation risky in situations with ambiguous or sensitive context
Prefers human oversight for critical incident response and decision-making
Worries that over-automation could erode vital analytical skills

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and critical thinking skills
Routine data processing skills may become less important due to automation
Interpersonal and communication skills remain essential for collaboration
Sees the need to upskill in areas like AI prompt engineering and data validation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning how to frame effective prompts for LLMs is a new responsibility
Needs to interpret and audit AI-generated outputs for accuracy
Responsible for integrating AI findings into traditional analysis workflows
Staying informed about AI ethics and potential biases is increasingly important

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Anticipates some routine analysis tasks could be replaced by AI
Not concerned about AI fully replacing the analyst role due to need for judgment
Views AI as a tool to augment, not eliminate, human analysts
Mildly concerned about job changes if AI adoption accelerates rapidly

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-driven outputs are reviewed by a human analyst before dissemination
Manual correction and flagging are built into report workflows
AI tools provide confidence scores to guide human review
Override mechanisms are mandated by company policy for critical decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has recently updated policies to encourage responsible AI use
Mandatory training on AI ethics and usage has been introduced
Team is piloting new AI tools with oversight from management
Increased emphasis on documentation and audit trails for AI-generated reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will reshape intelligence analysis by increasing speed and scale
Expects AI to improve threat detection but also introduce new security risks
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to analytical capabilities
Concerns about misuse of AI for misinformation or surveillance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes transparency in AI workflows is critical
Worries about algorithmic bias affecting analysis outcomes
Supports clear accountability for decisions involving AI outputs
Emphasizes the importance of protecting sensitive data in AI systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would like automated cross-referencing of multiple data sources in real time
Desires AI tools with built-in bias detection and explainability features
Sees value in smarter summarization tailored to target audiences
Prefers seamless integration of AI outputs into existing reporting platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects shift toward higher-level analysis and decision-making roles
Predicts increased collaboration between analysts and AI specialists
Anticipates more focus on validating and contextualizing AI findings
Envisions routine analytical tasks becoming fully automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in continuous training for analysts on new AI capabilities
Establish clear guidelines for responsible and ethical AI use
Regularly audit AI systems for accuracy and bias
Promote open dialogue between analysts, IT, and compliance on AI adoption
